coredata.dc:title: Sustainable agar-based film with zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (ZnO/CQD) nanocomposite for photocatalytic antimicrobial and antioxidant packaging of chicken breast

Kang, Jun-Won
coredata.dc:description: The rising need for sustainable food packaging has spurred the creation of biopolymer-based films with improved functionality. However, many conventional biopolymer films exhibit poor mechanical strength and oxidative stability, limiting their practical use in real food packaging. In this research, functional biopolymer-based films were developed by incorporating ZnO and zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (ZnO/CQD) nanocomposites into agar at 4 wt% and 8 wt%, respectively, using a green synthesis method involving chestnut shell. The Agar +8 %-ZnO/CQD (8 %) film (8ZCA8) enhanced the dispersion within the agar matrix, attributed to the presence of CQDs, facilitating a more homogeneous film structure and bolstering mechanical strength and barrier properties. In photocatalytic antimicrobial activity against Escherichia coli, Salmonella Typhimurium, and Listeria monocytogenes, the 8ZCA8 film achieved more than a 3-log CFU/g reduction on inoculated film surfaces and approximately a 2-log reduction on contaminated chicken breast under blue light irradiation. It also showed 13–20 % higher DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity than the ZnO-only film (ZA8). Moreover, the 8ZCA8 film exhibited lower cytotoxicity than the ZA8 film. When applied to the storage of chicken breast at 4 °C, the 8ZCA8 film demonstrated the highest photocatalytic antimicrobial activity and antioxidant performance, significantly outperforming the pristine agar film. These results highlight its potential as an effective active packaging material with antioxidant and antimicrobial functionalities, while also being environmentally friendly and sustainable.

head.ce:title: Sustainable agar-based film with zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (ZnO/CQD) nanocomposite for photocatalytic antimicrobial and antioxidant packaging of chicken breast

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rising need for sustainable food packaging has spurred the creation of biopolymer-based films with improved functionality. However, many conventional biopolymer films exhibit poor mechanical strength and oxidative stability, limiting their practical use in real food packaging. In this research, functional biopolymer-based films were developed by incorporating ZnO and zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (ZnO/CQD) nanocomposites into agar at 4 wt% and 8 wt%, respectively, using a green synthesis method involving chestnut shell. The Agar +8 %-ZnO/CQD (8 %) film (8ZCA8) enhanced the dispersion within the agar matrix, attributed to the presence of CQDs, facilitating a more homogeneous film structure and bolstering mechanical strength and barrier properties. In photocatalytic antimicrobial activity against Escherichia coli, Salmonella Typhimurium, and Listeria monocytogenes, the 8ZCA8 film achieved more than a 3-log CFU/g reduction on inoculated film surfaces and approximately a 2-log reduction on contaminated chicken breast under blue light irradiation. It also showed 13–20 % higher DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity than the ZnO-only film (ZA8). Moreover, the 8ZCA8 film exhibited lower cytotoxicity than the ZA8 film. When applied to the storage of chicken breast at 4 °C, the 8ZCA8 film demonstrated the highest photocatalytic antimicrobial activity and antioxidant performance, significantly outperforming the pristine agar film. These results highlight its potential as an effective active packaging material with antioxidant and antimicrobial functionalities, while also being environmentally friendly and sustainable.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chicken breast is widely consumed globally due to its high nutritional value, low fat content, and affordability (Karaca et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2022). Its high protein content and abundance of polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) render it susceptible to oxidative reactions, leading to protein and lipid oxidation, which can deteriorate the meat's flavor, texture, and nutritional value (Criado et al., 2020; Lund et al., 2007). Moreover, during its storage and distribution, chicken breast is particularly susceptible to pathogen contamination, posing a risk of foodborne illnesses if not effectively managed (Abebe et al., 2020). Hence, addressing the issues of oxidation and microbial contamination during storage and distribution is crucial for maintaining freshness (Priyadarshi et al., 2024).

Active packaging has gained increasing attention in recent years as an innovative approach to extending the shelf life and preserving the quality of food products. It incorporates specific functional agents such as antioxidants, antimicrobials, ethylene scavengers, oxygen scavengers, and moisture absorbers, enabling the packaging to actively interact with the food or its environment to maintain quality and safety (Ahmed et al., 2022). By incorporating specific additives into packaging materials, this method introduces additional functionalities such as antioxidant release, antimicrobial activity, and moisture control, thereby ensuring product safety and prolonging freshness (Argyri et al., 2012; Wani et al., 2025). Among these advancements, the use of nanoparticles represents a significant breakthrough due to their multifunctional properties. Their exceptional surface area-to-volume ratio enables effective antimicrobial and antioxidative effects even at minimal concentrations. Additionally, nanoparticles (NPs) offer UV protection, mechanical reinforcement, and direct contact-based interactions with food surfaces that contribute to microbial inhibition and antioxidant effects (Ashfaq et al., 2022; Upadhyay et al., 2024). To improve compatibility and safety in food contact materials, biocompatible fillers such as starch NPs, cellulose nanocrystals, and chitosan NPs have also been explored. These natural nanomaterials can reduce toxicity concerns while maintaining functional performance (Y. Chen, Jiang, et al., 2024; Garavand et al., 2022; Mu et al., 2019).

Biopolymers are increasingly recognized as sustainable alternatives to conventional petroleum-based plastics due to their biodegradability, non-toxicity, and reduced environmental impact (Zhang, Roy, et al., 2023). Originating from natural sources such as polysaccharides, proteins, and aliphatic polyesters, these materials are extensively utilized in food packaging for their ability to diminish plastic waste (Perera et al., 2023). Among various biopolymers, polysaccharides are especially valued for their availability, biocompatibility, biodegradability and film-forming properties (Kumar et al., 2019). In addition to these benefits, their inherent biodegradability contributes to environmental sustainability. Representative examples include cellulose and starch, which are widely studied for functional food packaging applications (Y. Chen, Wang, et al., 2024). Agar, derived from red seaweeds, is notable for its robust gelling properties, as it requires no additional ions or chemical crosslinkers to form stable gels, unlike other polysaccharides such as alginate, carrageenan, and starch (Sudha et al., 2014). It forms strong and stable gels at low concentrations, exhibits exceptional thermal hysteresis with distinct gelation and melting temperatures, and retains its gel strength and clarity under a variety of conditions, including wide temperature ranges and varying pH levels (Perera et al., 2024). Despite its benefits, agar films are limited by their brittleness, reduced mechanical strength, and high oxygen permeability, which can impact their effectiveness in food preservation (Arham et al., 2016; Roy et al., 2023). Moreover, sensitivity to humidity may compromise their structural integrity, increasing their vulnerability to microbial contamination (Roy et al., 2023).

Zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) have been extensively integrated into various biopolymers, including carboxymethyl cellulose, agar, pectin/alginate, carrageenan, and chitosan, due to their capability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) for antimicrobial activity and their expansive specific surface area, which enhances interactions with biopolymers, thus improving both antimicrobial efficacy and mechanical strength. (Al-Naamani et al., 2016; Espitia et al., 2016; Zhang, Sani, et al., 2023).

Recently, we successfully synthesized zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (ZnO/CQD) nanocomposites through a green synthesis process using a chestnut shell, a food byproduct, as a raw material. These nanocomposites were designed to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of ZnO, and we confirmed that their photocatalytic antimicrobial activity was significantly improved when coupled with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (Na & Kang, 2025). Furthermore, CQDs, nanoscale carbon-based materials, are recognized for their high antioxidant capacity, hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility, attributes due to their surface functional groups that enable effective free radical scavenging (Riahi et al., 2022; Singh et al., 2024). Leveraging these attributes, CQDs are expected to significantly boost the antioxidant properties of the composite film. Moreover, their inherent hydrophilicity is likely to promote better integration into the agar matrix, facilitating a more uniform dispersion, which is anticipated to enhance the mechanical strength and flexibility of the film. This uniformity is expected to improve adhesion to food surfaces and ensure consistent delivery of antimicrobial and antioxidant effects, thereby augmenting preservation. Additionally, the high biocompatibility of CQDs may decrease cytotoxicity compared to ZnO NPs alone, positioning this composite film as a safer option for food packaging applications.

Despite these anticipated advantages of CQDs, previous studies on active packaging have primarily focused on ZnO NPs alone (Zare et al., 2022). To our knowledge, this is the first study to incorporate eco-friendly synthesized ZnO/CQD nanocomposites the development of active packaging films specifically evaluated on real food (chicken breast). In addition to demonstrating antimicrobial and antioxidant activities, the present work provides a comprehensive evaluation of mechanical, barrier, cytotoxicity, and biodegradability properties, thereby offering a more holistic perspective on the practical applicability of sustainable food packaging. Accordingly, this study aimed to investigate whether ZnO/CQD-integrated agar films, derived from chestnut shell, could function as multifunctional active packaging. By focusing on their performance in preserving chicken breast, this work addresses practical challenges in food quality maintenance and sustainable packaging development.

Agar powder, potato dextrose agar (PDA), tryptic soy broth (TSB), peptone water (PW; NIER), Sorbitol MacConkey Agar, Xylose Lysine Deoxycholate Agar, and Oxford Agar Base with Oxford antimicrobial supplement were all purchased from MBcell (Seoul, Korea). Potassium persulfate, 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), 2,2′-Azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 2-thiobarbituric acid (TBA), 1,1,3,3-tetraethoxyl propane (TEP), and 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Trichloroacetic acid (TCA), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ethanol, ethyl acetate, and guanidine hydrochloride were supplied by Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Korea). Glycerol was sourced from Samchun Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Seoul, Korea). Whatman No.1 filter papers were obtained from Whatman plc (Maidstone, UK). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 20 × concentrate, pH 7.4) was purchased from HanLAB Corp. (Bucheon, Korea), diluted to 1 × using distilled water (DW), and sterilized by autoclaving prior to use. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and penicillin-streptomycin were purchased from Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA), while fetal bovine serum (FBS) was sourced from Atlas Biologicals (Fort Collins, CO, USA). MTT reagent was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). The bacterial strains Escherichia coli O157:H7 (ATCC 43890, 43889, 35150), Salmonella typhimurium (DT 104, ATCC 19585, 19115), Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313, 27649, 25923), and the Caco-2 human colon adenocarcinoma cell line were all sourced from the Food Microbial Laboratory at Dongguk University (Gyeonggi-do, Korea). Fresh chicken breast samples (Harim brand, Korea) were purchased from a local supermarket in Goyang, Gyeonggi-do, Korea, and used immediately upon arrival. Aspergillus niger spores for biodegradability testing were obtained from the Korean Collection for Type Cultures (KCTC, Jeongeup, Korea) and cultured on PDA medium (MBcell, Seoul, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of agar-based films containing ZnO or ZnO/CQD nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The ZnO NPs and 8 %-ZnO/CQD nanocomposite were synthesized following the protocol outlined by Na et al. (2025). A molten agar solution (100 mL; 2.5 % w/v) was prepared by dissolving 2.5 g of agar powder in 100 mL of ultrapure distilled water and distributed into five 500 mL beakers. The mixture was heated at 95 °C for 30 min with constant stirring at 1000 rpm. Subsequently, 100 mg and 200 mg of ZnO NPs or 8 %-ZnO/CQD were added to the beakers containing 4 % (w/w) and 8 % (w/w) agar, respectively. A control sample was prepared in a separate beaker without nanoparticles. The samples were further heated at 95 °C for 1 h, with continued mixing at 1000 rpm. To all samples, 1.5 g of glycerol (60 % w/w of agar) was added as a plasticizer. The mixtures were heated until they reached a sticky liquid consistency, approximately 10 min, and 7 mL of the final film-forming solution was promptly poured into Petri dishes (90
×
15 mm). The films were dried in an oven at 50 °C for 48 h, removed from the plates, and stored in airtight plastic bags for further analysis. ZnO + Agar (4 %) is referred to as ZA4, ZnO + Agar (8 %) as ZA8, 8 %-ZnO/CQD + Agar (4 %) as 8ZCA4, and 8 %-ZnO/CQD + Agar (8 %) as 8ZCA8.

The morphological and compositional characteristics of the films were examined using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM; JEOL-7800F, JEOL Ltd, Japan) in conjunction with an Energy Dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS; JEOL-7800F, JEOL Ltd, Japan). The crystalline structure of the films was analyzed using high-resolution X-ray diffraction (HR-XRD, SmartLab, Rigaku, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed with an FT-IR spectrometer (INVENIO, BRUKER, Germany) to determine the chemical structure of the films. Each sample was baseline-corrected individually prior to measurement to ensure accurate spectral acquisition. The UV-blocking and opacity of the films were evaluated using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). For these measurements, film samples were cut and attached to one side of a quartz cuvette. UV-blocking properties were assessed by measuring the transmittance in the 200–400 nm range, while opacity was determined by measuring the absorbance at 600 nm. Thermal stability was assessed through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DSC), performed with a simultaneous DSC/TGA instrument (SDT Q600, TA Instruments, USA). The color of the films was measured using a colorimeter (NE 4000, Nippon Denshoku, Japan) by recording the color parameters L∗ (lightness), a∗ (red/green), and b∗ (yellow/blue).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of the agar-based films

ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the films was measured using a digital electronic micrometer (293-240-30; Mitutoyo, Japan) with an accuracy of 0.001 mm and a resolution range of 0–25 mm. Thickness measurements were taken at five randomly selected positions on each film sample, with each measurement repeated three times. The average thickness values for each sample were calculated and subsequently used for the determination of tensile strength (TS).

The moisture content and water solubility of the films (2
×
2 cm) were measured using the gravimetric method (Candra et al., 2023). The films were initially weighed before drying (Wi), and the final drying (Wf) was carried out at 100 °C in an oven with silica gel (Home planet, Coupang, Seoul, Korea) until a constant weight was achieved. The dried films were then immersed in ultrapure distilled water for 24 h. Subsequently, excess water was removed using filter paper (Whatman No.1), and the films were dried again at 100 °C in an oven with silica gel until a constant weight (Wd) was attained. The moisture content (MC) and water solubility (WS) of the films were then calculated using equations (1) and (2).

To evaluate the mechanical properties of the films, samples were cut into 1 cm
×
5 cm strips for testing. Tensile strength (TS; MPa) and percent elongation at break (EAB; %) were measured using a texture analyzer (PLUS-UPGRADE, Stable Micro Systems Ltd, UK). Initial grip separation and extension speed were set at 30 mm and 5 mm/s, respectively. Experiments were performed at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C) and an average relative humidity of approximately 56 %. Three independent measurements were performed for each film sample to ensure accuracy.

TS and EAB can be calculated using Equations (3) and (4), respectively. Important factors include Fmax, which is the maximum force required to break the film (N), A, the surface area (m2), l
0 , the initial grip length of the film (mm), and ΔL, which is the film length at rupture.
Oxygen permeability was assessed using an oxygen transmittance tester (OX-TRAN Model 2/21, Ametek Mocon, USA) at 23 °C, with an N2 flow rate of 10 sccm, across a 5 cm2 area, spanning 15 cycles.

Surface wettability of the films was quantified using a Pendant Drop Tensiometer (DSA100, KRÜSS, Germany) employing the dripping method with 5 μL of DW. The contact angle was recorded at 60 s post-application to the film surfaces. Experiments were performed at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C) and an average relative humidity of approximately 56 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant properties of the agar-based films
Radical scavenging activity after food contact
ce:section.ce:para:
Antioxidant capacities of the films were assessed using methods modified from Yang et al. (2024) and Tang et al. (2022). Initially, a 0.1 mM solution of DPPH was prepared in methanol. Film samples (0.1 g) were wrapped above chicken breast samples and stored at 4 °C. At predetermined time points (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 days), the films were removed and directly immersed in 4 mL of this solution, forming two groups: one illuminated with blue light and one kept dark. The reaction mixes were incubated in darkness for 1 h to avoid photo-degradation. Absorbance was subsequently measured at 517 nm using a UV–Vis spectrometer, and DPPH scavenging activity was calculated with the following formula:
(5)
DPPH
scavenging
activity
( % )
=
[
(
A 0
−
A 1
)
/
A 0
]
× 100
where A0 represents the absorbance of the DPPH solution without the sample, and A1 represents the absorbance of the DPPH solution with the film sample.

ABTS radicals were synthesized by mixing 7 mM ABTS with 2.45 mM potassium persulfate and allowing the mixture to sit for 16 h in darkness. A working solution was then created by diluting these radicals with PBS to achieve an absorbance of 0.8 at 734 nm. The film sample (0.1 g) was incubated in 4 mL of the ABTS solution for 1 h in darkness. Absorbance was measured at 734 nm using a UV–Vis spectrometer, and the ABTS free radical scavenging rate was determined using the following equation:
(6)
ABTS
scavenging
activity
( % )
=
[
(
A 0
–
A S
)
/
A 0
]
× 100
where A0 is the absorbance of the ABTS solution without the sample, and AS is the absorbance of the sample in the ABTS solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radical scavenging activity of the agar-based film extract
ce:section.ce:para: For extract-based antioxidant analysis, films were incubated in PBS at a ratio of 1 cm2/mL at 4 °C for 24 h. After extraction, 1 mL of the extract was mixed with 3 mL of the respective radical solution. The mixture was incubated in the dark for 1 h at room temperature. Absorbance was measured at 517 nm for DPPH or 734 nm for ABTS using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and radical scavenging activity was calculated using Equations (5) and (6) described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro analysis of the agar-based films

ce:section.ce:para: Strains of Escherichia coli O157:H7 (ATCC 43890, ATCC 43889, and ATCC 35150), Salmonella Typhimurium (DT 104, ATCC 19585, and ATCC 19115), and Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313, ATCC 27649, and ATCC 25923) were sourced from the Food Microbial Laboratory at Dongguk University (Gyeonggi-do, Republic of Korea) for the study. Each strain was cultured separately in 5 mL of TSB at 37 °C for 24 h. The cells were harvested by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 20 min, washed twice with PBS, and resuspended in PBS. These suspensions were then mixed to prepare a cocktail with concentrations ranging from 107-108 CFU/mL for each pathogen. The cocktail served as the inoculum for the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial efficacy of the agar-based films under 405 nm blue light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: A constant electric current of 1.02 A from a DC power source (TDP-1001B, TOYOTECH, Korea) powered four blue LED chips (LG Innotek Co., Seoul, Korea), each emitting a wavelength of 405 nm. The intensity of the light emitted was set to 31.66 mW/cm2 at a distance of 5 cm from the source.

The experiment was conducted using the plate counting method. Samples were prepared by placing 2
×
2 cm pieces of films (Agar, ZA4, ZA8, 8ZCA4, 8ZCA8) in Petri dishes (50
×
15 mm). Each film was inoculated with 10 μL of the cocktail, divided over 5 drops, and placed 5 cm from the blue light source for exposure to a dose range of 56.99–113.98 J/cm2. A control group was kept in the dark. Post irradiation, the films were transferred to stomacher bags containing 10 mL of 0.2 % PW and agitated for 2 min using a stomacher (BagMixer 400, Interscience, France). Subsequently, 1 mL from each film was serially diluted in 9 mL of PW, and 0.1 mL of these dilutions was plated on selective media. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, typical colonies on Sorbitol MacConkey Agar, Xylose Lysine Deoxycholate agar, and Oxford Agar Base with Oxford antimicrobial supplement were counted and quantified as log10 CFU/mL.

In parallel, film extracts were prepared by immersing each film (1 cm2/mL) in PBS and incubating at 4 °C for 24 h. To confirm whether ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD were released into the extract, 5 mL of film extract was inoculated with 100 μL of the cocktail and placed 5 cm from the blue light source for exposure to a dose range of 19.57–49.83 J/cm2. At each interval corresponding to 19.57 J/cm2, 1 mL of the reaction mixture was sampled, serially diluted in 9 mL of PW, and 0.1 mL of the dilution was plated on the same selective media. Colony enumeration was performed after incubation at 37 °C for 24 h and expressed as log10 CFU/mL.

Caco-2 cell lines were cultured in a medium consisting of 80 % DMEM, 20 % heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum, and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. Prior to evaluating cellular toxicity, 1 cm2 of ZA8 and 8ZCA8 films were sterilized using ethanol and PBS, followed by exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light for 24 h. The films were then extracted at a concentration of 1 cm2/mL, and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with complete medium.

To assess cellular toxicity, 1
×
104 Caco-2 cells were seeded into 96 -well cell culture plates and allowed 24 h for cell attachment. Following this period, the medium was substituted with various concentrations (25 %, 50 %, 75 %, 100 %) of film extracts (ZA8 and 8ZCA8), with untreated cell culture medium serving as the negative control. After 24 h, the extract solutions were replaced with 100 μL of MTT medium (0.5 mg/mL) and were further incubated for 4 h. The MTT medium was removed and substituted with 100 μL of DMSO to dissolve the formazan crystals, and the absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (GlowMax Explorer with Luminescence and Fluorescence, Promega, USA). Cell viability was calculated as follows:
(7)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
t h e
t r e a t e d
w e l l s
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
t h e
c o n t r o l
w e l l s
× 100

Fresh chicken breasts were acquired from Harim (Korea), cut into 5 g pieces, and immediately utilized for the experiments. The prepared samples underwent Color measurement, Thiobarbituric acid reagent substances (TBARS) analysis, and determination of carbonyl content. For photocatalytic antimicrobial analysis, samples were cut into 2
×
2 cm pieces and were also immediately used in the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color evaluation of chicken breast before and after agar-based film packaging
ce:section.ce:para: The color of both coated and uncoated chicken breast samples was analyzed using a color meter (NE 4000, Nippon Denshoku, Japan). Each sample was wrapped with the film (Agar, ZA8, or 8ZCA8) and stored at 4 °C. Additionally, some of the film-wrapped samples were irradiated with 405 nm blue light at a dose of 455.92 J/cm2 before storage. After film removal, color values for L∗ (lightness), a∗ (red/green), and b∗ (yellow/blue) were determined on days 1 and 10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic antimicrobial activity analysis under 405 nm blue light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
Samples were prepared by placing 2
×
2 cm pieces of chicken breast onto which 10 μL of inoculum was applied in 5 separate drops, and then wrapped with the respective films (Agar, ZA4, ZA8, 8ZCA4, 8ZCA8). The samples were then exposed to blue light at a distance of 5 cm from the light source, as described in section 2.6.1.2, with doses ranging from 113.98 to 455.92 J/cm2. The post-treatment process was carried out in accordance with the procedure outlined in section 2.6.1.2.

Lipid oxidation in both control and coated chicken samples was determined using the method described by Criado et al. (2020). As detailed in section 2.7.1, lipid oxidation in chicken breast samples was evaluated after 21 days of storage at 4 °C under refrigerated conditions. After the films were removed, 5 g of chicken breast was homogenized with 25 mL of ultrapure distilled water and 5 mL of a 15 % (w/w) TCA solution. The mixture was centrifuged, and 2 mL of the supernatant was combined with 0.5 mL of a 0.2 mM TBA solution and incubated for 90 min at 95 °C. Prior to incubation, the TBA solution was preheated to 50 °C. The absorbance at 520 nm was subsequently measured using a microplate reader (AMR-100, Hangzhou Allsheng Instruments Co., Ltd, China). MDA content was quantified based on a standard curve using TEP.

Protein oxidation in control and coated chicken breast samples was assessed by measuring the carbonyl content using a modified procedure described by Nawaz et al. (2024). As detailed in section 2.7.1, chicken breast samples were packaged and analyzed bi-daily over a 10-day period. Control samples were packaged solely in film without blue light treatment, while treated samples were exposed to 455.92 J/cm2 of blue light prior to storage at 4 °C under refrigerated conditions. Briefly, after film removal, 5 g of chicken breast was homogenized in 50 mL of 20 mM PBS. A 1 mL aliquot of this homogenate was mixed with 5 mL of 10 % TCA (w/v) and incubated on ice for 15 min to precipitate proteins. The precipitate was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and the supernatant discarded. The pellet was washed again with 5 mL of 10 % TCA, incubated on ice for 15 min, and then centrifuged under identical conditions. Subsequently, the precipitate was treated with 2.5 mL of 2 mg/mL DNPH in 2 M HCl and incubated in the dark for 1 h. A blank sample was prepared using 0.4 mL of 2 M HCl instead of DNPH. The reaction was halted by addition of 2.5 mL of 20 % TCA, followed by a 15-min incubation on ice and centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The pellet was subsequently washed three times with 1 mL of ethanol and ethyl acetate in a 1:1 (v/v) ratio to remove unreacted DNPH. After air-drying for 15 min in a fume hood, the pellet was dissolved in 1.5 mL of 20 mM phosphate buffer containing 6 M guanidine hydrochloride (pH 6.5) and incubated for 30 min. Insoluble residues were removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The absorbance of the supernatant was read at 370 nm, and the protein carbonyl content was quantified using the following procedure:
(8)
Carbonyl
( mmol )
/
protein
( mg )
=
[
A 370
/
(
A 280
−
A 370
× 0.43
)
× 22000
]
×
10 6

The biodegradability of the film samples was assessed using a modified ASTM G21–09 method (Candra et al., 2023; Fernandez-Bats et al., 2018). A spore suspension (10 μL) of Aspergillus niger was inoculated onto PDA in 90 × 15 mm Petri dishes. Subsequently, films (2 × 2 cm) were cut and placed directly over the inoculated surface. The plates were incubated at 30 °C and 60 % relative humidity for 14 days. Film degradation was monitored visually and recorded via image documentation at designated time points to evaluate fungal colonization and structural breakdown.

Experimental data were captured in triplicate. All statistical analyses were carried out using the Kruskal-Wallis test, with the Bonferroni test as a post-hoc analysis when Kruskal-Wallis results indicated statistical significance. A P-value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant in all instances. All analyses were performed using the Statistical Analysis System (SAS) version 9.4 (SAS Institute inc., Cary, NC, USA).

Fig. 1 presents the FE-SEM images and EDS spectrum, characterizing the surface morphology and elemental composition of Agar, ZA8, and 8ZC8 films. The Agar film, devoid of nanoparticles, exhibits a smooth surface free of noticeable defects. In contrast, the ZA8 and 8ZCA8 films, containing nanoparticles, show surface defects attributed to these particles. The EDS spectrum confirms the elemental distribution within the films, demonstrating that the nanoparticles are well dispersed, as evidenced by the FE-SEM images (Fig. 1c and e). A detailed review of the FE-SEM images of ZA8 and 8ZCA8 indicates that the aggregated nanoparticles tend to disperse uniformly, a phenomenon common in films with metal oxide nanoparticles. Research suggests an increase in aggregation with rising nanoparticle concentration (Purohit et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2021). Specifically, the dispersion in Fig. 1b–e of the aggregated ZnO and 8 %-ZnO/CQD is likely due to the properties of metal oxide nanoparticles. However, 8ZCA8 displays a more uniform dispersion than ZA8, because hydrophilic CQD wrapping around ZnO NPs enhances interaction with the agar matrix, improving dispersion (Yi et al., 2024).

Crystalline and chemical structures of films were examined using XRD and FTIR (Fig. 2 a and b). The XRD patterns of the Agar, ZA8, and 8ZCA8 films, as shown in Fig. 2a, exhibit peaks at 13° and 19°, indicating the organized structure and semi-crystalline nature of the agar polymer matrix (Arfat et al., 2017). The XRD patterns of ZA8 and 8ZCA8 films reveal eight primary diffraction peaks characteristic of ZnO, corresponding to various crystal planes: (1 0 0), (0 0 2), (1 0 1), (1 0 2), (1 1 0), (1 0 3), (2 0 0), and (1 1 2).

FTIR spectra of Agar, ZA8, and 8ZCA8, depicted in Fig. 2b, exhibit absorption bands at 1029, 1407, 1659, 2878, 2935, and 3285 cm−1, attributed to (C-H or CH3), -C=O, C=O, stretching type CH3, asymmetrical stretching CH3, and O-H (Kumar et al., 2019). It was observed that hydrophobic functional groups such as (C-H or CH3), stretching type CH3, and asymmetrical stretching CH3 (Schmidt et al., 2006) were more prominent than hydrophilic groups like O-H and C-O (Guo et al., 2019). Consequently, the films were anticipated to exhibit hydrophobic properties. The comparison of FTIR spectra between Agar, ZA8, and 8ZCA8 films highlighted differences in peak intensity; however, the unchanged peak positions indicate that no significant structural alterations occurred due to the interaction between ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD and agar.

The appearance characteristics of food packaging films, especially transparency, play a crucial role in consumer acceptance by facilitating the visual inspection of food quality (Cazón et al., 2018). However, nanocomposite films containing nanoparticles typically exhibit a reduction in transparency proportional to the nanoparticle concentration, necessitating adjustments to the nanoparticle levels to preserve the ability to visually assess food quality (Guzman-Puyol et al., 2022; Saedi et al., 2021). Therefore, it is imperative to achieve a balance between functionality and visual clarity.

Apart from transparency, the UV-blocking capability of food packaging is essential for protecting contents from external stressors, as excessive UV exposure can hasten oxidation and microbial degradation (Sani et al., 2021). As demonstrated in Fig. 4 , the incorporation of ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD significantly enhanced the UV-blocking proficiency of the films relative to the plain Agar film. Notably, 8ZCA8 displayed the most effective UV-blocking ability, which may be attributed to ZnO's inherent UV absorption properties, potentially augmented by the presence of CQDs that increase light absorption and scattering (Hess et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2015).

The integration of ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD into agar-based films significantly enhanced their mechanical properties, oxygen barrier function, and hydrophobicity. Although glycerol was used as a plasticizer, which typically weakens hydrogen bonding and increases the free volume within polymer matrices (Candra et al., 2023; Fransiska et al., 2024), the addition of ZnO and particularly 8 %-ZnO/CQD counteracted these effects by reinforcing the film structure. ZnO and 8 %-ZnO/CQD also facilitated stronger intermolecular interactions within the agar matrix, resulting in increased tensile strength (TS) and reduced oxygen transmission rate (OTR). Particularly, substantial improvements were most evident in the 8ZCA8 film, indicating that CQD plays a crucial role in further optimizing these properties. The enhanced performance of the 8ZCA8 compared to ZA8 can be attributed to the hydrophilic nature of CQD, which promotes better ZnO dispersion and strengthens interfacial interactions with the agar matrix (Riahi et al., 2022). This uniform distribution of nanoparticles contributes to a more cohesive film network, thereby increasing tensile strength and reducing oxygen permeability (Khalaj et al., 2016). Furthermore, the presence of CQD modified the surface energy of the film, leading to increased hydrophobicity (Jayakumar et al., 2024). This alteration in surface properties likely reduced molecular mobility and water affinity, potentially explaining the observed increase in WCA values. These structural changes are supported by FTIR spectra and SEM images (Figs. 1 and 2b), showing improved ZnO dispersion and a more compact film network in the 8ZCA8 formulation. As a result, these changes induced by CQD led to a denser, more robust film matrix, achieving superior mechanical strength, enhanced oxygen barrier performance, and increased hydrophobicity. Given these improvements, further analyses were carried out to assess their impact on moisture resistance through MC and WS measurements.

Maintaining low WS and optimal MC is essential for the longevity and effectiveness of food packaging films. These measures help prevent degradation due to moisture exposure while preserving mechanical strength and barrier properties, contributing significantly to product protection and extended shelf life (Sanyang et al., 2016). As indicated in Table 3, the Agar film exhibited the highest MC, whereas ZA4 and ZA8 slightly reduced MC and WS, albeit not significantly. Notably, 8ZCA4 and 8ZCA8 demonstrated the lowest MC and WS values, with 8ZCA8 showing a significant reduction compared to both the Agar and ZA8 films (p < 0.05). These observations are consistent with earlier findings on mechanical strength, oxygen permeability, and hydrophobicity, which support the hypothesis that CQD enhances structural integrity through improved interfacial interactions. The formation of additional hydrogen bonds between functional groups on the CQD surface (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) and the agar matrix's hydroxyl groups likely contributed to this effect by reducing molecular mobility and limiting water absorption (Atef et al., 2014; Candra et al., 2023). Consequently, the reduction in MC and WS observed in 8ZCA8 confirms that the structural modifications induced by CQD improved moisture resistance.

Thermal stability is a crucial parameter for biopolymer films, as they are inherently fragile and must resist heat exposure during processing, storage, and transportation to maintain the integrity of the packaged food (Bhatia et al., 2024). To assess the thermal properties of the films, TGA and DSC analyses were performed (Fig. 6 ). The TGA curves displayed three stages of weight loss: the initial (30–100 °C) stage arose from moisture loss, the mid-stage (100–300 °C) from the degradation of polymer functional groups, and the final stage (300–1000 °C) from nanoparticle decomposition (Fig. 6a) (J. Chen et al., 2022). The DSC results corroborated these stages, demonstrating consistency between the two analyses (Fig. 6b). The addition of ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD improved the thermal stability of the films, consistent with previous research that showed enhanced thermal stability when nanoparticles were incorporated into agar-based films (Kumar et al., 2019; Shankar & Rhim, 2017; Shukla et al., 2012). Notably, 8ZCA8 exhibited superior thermal stability, likely because CQD reduced ZnO's thermal decomposition and enhanced the nanocomposite's stability (Malitha et al., 2024; Navidfar et al., 2022). Additionally, the hydrophilic nature of CQDs facilitated a uniform dispersion within the agar matrix, enhancing interfacial interactions. In contrast, ZnO alone may have dispersed less effectively, leading to weaker structural integrity. The FTIR spectra and SEM images support this, showing improved dispersion of ZnO and a denser film network in 8ZCA8 (Figs. 1 and 2a).

Antimicrobial properties in food packaging are essential to inhibit the growth of spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms, thereby ensuring food safety and extending shelf life. For this purpose, antimicrobial agents are incorporated using various techniques, such as embedding volatile compounds, coating surfaces with active substances, or utilizing polymers with inherent antimicrobial activity (Othman et al., 2014).

To evaluate the photocatalytic antimicrobial activity of the films, we conducted an experiment simulating contamination on the film surface with foodborne pathogens and contamination on the food surface after packaging with the film. The results of these simulations are presented in Fig. 7 a–c for contaminated film surfaces and Fig. 7d–f for contaminated food surfaces, with the corresponding dark condition results shown in Fig. 7g and h. Both simulations—contamination of the film surface and contamination occurring between the film and the food—demonstrated that films containing 8 %-ZnO/CQD exhibited greater antimicrobial effects than those with ZnO alone (Fig. 7a–f). Among them, 8ZCA8 achieved the most significant results, demonstrating approximately a 5- to 6-log reduction on film surfaces with a blue light dose of 113.98 J/cm2, while food surfaces required a higher dose of 455.92 J/cm2 to achieve a 4- to 5 -log reduction. This disparity may arise from the physical differences between the surfaces: the smooth and uniform structure of the film surface likely facilitates better light penetration and more efficient reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, whereas the uneven texture and organic matter on the food surface may impede these processes, potentially reducing overall antimicrobial efficacy (Wang et al., 2024). The superior performance of 8ZCA8 aligns with our previous findings that CQD enhances the photocatalytic activity of ZnO by increasing surface defect sites and improving charge separation (Na et al., 2025). These mechanisms amplify ROS generation, enabling the films to more effectively disrupt microbial components such as proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids (McKenzie et al., 2016). In contrast, the limited antimicrobial effects observed on the Agar film under blue light treatment can be attributed to the modest ROS generation from endogenous photosensitizers such as porphyrins, which are naturally present in microorganisms (Amodeo et al., 2022). Interestingly, L. monocytogenes exhibited lower reduction rates compared to E. coli and S. Typhimurium in both contamination scenarios. This outcome could be attributed to differences in bacterial cell wall structure. As E. coli and S. Typhimurium are Gram-negative bacteria, they possess a complex but thin peptidoglycan layer, whereas L. monocytogenes is a Gram-positive bacterium with a thicker cell wall composed of multiple peptidoglycan layers (Anitha et al., 2012). Differences in the antimicrobial effects of films between Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria have been consistently reported in several studies (Alghamdi et al., 2022; Candra et al., 2023; J. Chen et al., 2022).

Overall, the results highlight that 8 %-ZnO/CQD films demonstrate the most effective photocatalytic antimicrobial activity, particularly on smooth surfaces. This suggests a promising strategy for enhancing food safety by inhibiting microbial growth under simulated packaging conditions. Such potential positions 8 %-ZnO/CQD films favorably for applications in active packaging, where their enhanced antimicrobial properties could significantly curb microbial proliferation. To verify the underlying mechanism, we further assessed whether the antimicrobial activity was contact-based or mediated by leaching (Fig. 7i–k). Film extracts prepared in PBS at 4 °C for 24 h showed no statistically significant differences in antimicrobial activity compared to the PBS control across all tested strains (E. coli, S. Typhimurium, and L. monocytogenes), supporting that the observed effects are primarily contact-dependent.

Lipid and protein oxidation critically affect the quality of chicken breast, with lipid oxidation leading to off-flavors, discoloration, and nutrient loss, while protein oxidation diminishes water-holding capacity, tenderness, and juiciness (Xiao et al., 2011). Due to their significant influence on meat quality, these oxidative changes were evaluated as quality indicators.

To determine the efficacy of the films in mitigating oxidative stress in actual food systems, TBARS and DNPH analyses evaluated lipid and protein oxidation levels in packaged chicken breast (Fig. 8 ). As depicted in Fig. 8a, the TBARS assay analyzed chicken breasts packaged with Agar, ZA8, and 8ZCA8 films, both immediately refrigerated and after exposure to 455.92 J/cm2 of blue light. The initial TBARS value of the chicken breast before refrigeration was approximately 10 ng/g, which confirmed the meat's freshness (Criado et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2020). After 21 days of refrigeration without blue light treatment, TBARS values increased significantly in all film-packaged samples compared to the initial values (p < 0.05). However, the TBARS values of the chicken breasts packaged with 8ZCA8 film were significantly lower than those with the Agar film, with the 8ZCA8 film demonstrating the smallest increase among the three films (p < 0.05). Meanwhile, for chicken breast refrigerated in the dark after blue light treatment, the TBARS value significantly increased in the chicken breasts packaged with the Agar film (p < 0.05). Conversely, the TBARS values for the chicken breasts packed with ZA8 or 8ZCA8 films did not increase significantly compared to the pre-storage levels (p > 0.05), suggesting that the ZA8 and 8ZCA8 films are potentially effective in inhibiting oxidation. Similarly, the extent of protein oxidation, measured as carbonyl content, also increased over the storage period across all samples (Fig. 8b). On Day 0, the carbonyl content was approximately 2 nmol/g protein for all samples, indicating a comparable baseline oxidative state prior to storage. By Day 10, samples packaged with the Agar film exhibited the highest carbonyl levels, while those packaged with the 8ZCA8 film showed distinctly lower levels. Moreover, films treated with blue light further mitigated protein oxidation, as indicated by the reduced carbonyl levels in samples packaged with treated films compared to untreated ones.

The DPPH and ABTS assays were employed to assess the free radical scavenging ability of the films after direct contact with chicken breast over a 10-day storage period (Fig. 9 ). Across all time points, no statistically significant changes were observed in the antioxidant activity of any film, indicating that their radical scavenging properties remained stable during storage. The results revealed that 8ZCA8 demonstrated the highest antioxidant activity, significantly surpassing the DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging capacities of the ZA8 and Agar films (p < 0.05). These findings affirm the superior antioxidative capacity of 8ZCA8 in preventing oxidation in packaged food. Additionally, blue light treatment did not significantly affect the antioxidant activity of the films.

Collectively, the TBARS and DNPH results indicate that the 8ZCA8 was the most effective at suppressing both lipid and protein oxidation, primarily because of its superior oxygen barrier properties and lower moisture content (Tables 2 and 3) (Andrade et al., 2023). Notably, 8ZCA8 demonstrated enhanced antioxidant activity compared to ZA8, attributable to the radical scavenging capabilities of CQD. The sp2-hybridized carbon core of CQD promotes efficient electron transfer and storage, and its surface functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl, serve as hydrogen donors, collectively enhancing ROS neutralization and free radical scavenging (Li et al., 2023). Moreover, the uniform dispersion of ZnO/CQD within the film matrix likely improved the film's structural integrity, thereby enhancing its protective function against oxidation. These findings confirm that blue light treatment did not significantly affect the oxidative stability, and the improvements observed were exclusively related to the characteristics of the film itself. Additionally, the DPPH and ABTS assays using film extracts (Fig. 9c and d) revealed negligible radical scavenging activity, suggesting that the antioxidant effect is not due to leached components. This observation is consistent with the extract-based photocatalytic antimicrobial results (Fig. 7i–k), further supporting that both antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of the films are primarily contact-dependent.

Moreover, since color changes are a critical indicator of freshness, the variation in the color of chicken breast packaged with the films was assessed during the storage period (Yousuf et al., 2018). Fig. 10 illustrates the visual differences, which are quantitatively supported by the corresponding colorimeter measurements summarized in Table 4 .

The analysis of L∗ revealed no significant differences among films at either time point, suggesting that the composition of the film had minimal impact on brightness. However, within the same film group, L∗ values declined significantly during storage in the Agar film, whereas those stored with ZA8 and 8ZCA8 displayed superior brightness retention. For a∗, no significant differences were detected among films, although all samples exhibited an increase in redness after storage. Since this trend was consistent throughout the samples, it is likely attributable to natural changes in the food. Regarding b∗, while all samples showed an increase in yellowness after storage, this enhancement may be linked to oxidative changes occurring during the storage period (Özünlü & Ergezer, 2022). Notably, the increase in b∗ was the smallest in chicken breasts stored with 8ZCA8, indicating enhanced color stability. This pattern is consistent with the results of oxidative stability shown in Figs. 8–9, where 8ZCA8 exhibited the most potent antioxidant properties, suggesting that its exceptional oxidative stability contributed to the minimal increase in b∗ values in this study.

Although ZnO in its bulk form is classified as a Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) substance, its nanoparticle form might exhibit different physicochemical properties, underscoring the need for a thorough biocompatibility assessment (Akbar & Sadiq, 2021). This emphasizes the necessity of evaluating the safety of films that incorporate ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD nanocomposites for potential applications in food packaging.

The safety of films made of ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD for use as food packaging was assessed by evaluating their cell viability, as depicted in Fig. 11 . The biocompatibility of the films was examined using the MTT assay with Caco-2 cells, and film extracts were tested at concentrations of 0 %, 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 %, using 0 % as the control. Extracts from both ZA8 and 8ZCA8 films showed a reduction in cell viability as the extract concentration increased. For ZA8 extracts, cell viability remained statistically similar to the control at a concentration of 25 %, while for 8ZCA8 extracts, it remained comparable up to 50 % (p > 0.05). At similar extract concentrations, cell viability between ZA8 and 8ZCA8 films showed a significant difference beginning at 50 %, demonstrating that the biocompatibility of 8ZCA8 is statistically superior to that of ZA8 (p < 0.05). The exact mechanism through which CQDs reduce the cytotoxicity of ZnO remains uncertain; however, it is likely that the intrinsic biocompatibility of CQDs enhances the surface properties of ZnO, thereby leading to improved biocompatibility (Mishra et al., 2024). CQDs may act as a protective layer, reducing direct interaction between the reactive surfaces of ZnO and cells, while also stabilizing the surface and minimizing localized oxidative stress. In accordance with GB/T 16886.5-2003 (IS 40993–5:1999), materials exhibiting a cell viability of 75 % or higher are classified as non-cytotoxic (Candra et al., 2023). These findings indicate that the films made using ZnO or 8 %-ZnO/CQD synthesized from chestnut shells are non-cytotoxic under these in vitro conditions. Nevertheless, given that cytotoxicity may differ in more complex biological environments, further in vivo studies are imperative to thoroughly evaluate their safety for food packaging applications.

Evaluating biodegradability is essential to ensure the environmental sustainability of packaging materials after disposal (Candra et al., 2023). To assess this, Aspergillus niger was selected as the test organism due to its ecological role as a soil-dwelling fungus capable of breaking down organic matter such as leaf litter and decaying plants. Through the secretion of extracellular enzymes, A. niger degrades complex compounds into simpler nutrients, playing a vital role in ecosystem nutrient cycling. This metabolic versatility makes A. niger a reliable model for evaluating the biodegradation of organic films (Fransiska et al., 2024).

In this study, the Agar, ZA8, and 8ZCA8 films exhibited more than 60 % surface coverage by A. niger after 10 days of incubation (Fig. 12 ), indicating active microbial colonization and enzymatic degradation under simulated environmental conditions. The substantial fungal growth observed across all film types suggests that the agar-based matrix provided an accessible carbon source for fungal metabolism. Importantly, the incorporation of ZnO and 8 %-ZnO/CQD nanocomposites did not inhibit fungal activity, demonstrating that these functional additives did not compromise the biodegradability of the films. Collectively, these findings indicate that the developed films maintain environmental compatibility while delivering additional functional benefits such as antimicrobial activity. The demonstrated biodegradability reinforces their potential as sustainable materials for food packaging applications, addressing both food safety and ecological concerns.

This study established that agar-based films containing 8 %-ZnO/CQD demonstrated superior antioxidant activity, photocatalytic antimicrobial activity, and enhanced physicochemical properties compared to those with ZnO alone. These improvements can be ascribed to the presence of CQD, which not only facilitated the dispersion of nanocomposites within the agar matrix owing to its hydrophilic nature but also contributed to the film's properties via its inherent antioxidant capacity and high biocompatibility. In addition, both antioxidant and antimicrobial activities were contact-dependent, highlighting the importance of direct interaction between the film surface and target components. Furthermore, the films were confirmed to be biodegradable, suggesting their potential as environmentally friendly alternatives to conventional plastic-based packaging. Given the favorable outcomes in preserving chicken breast, the 8ZCA8 demonstrates potential for application in active packaging, particularly for high-protein foods prone to oxidative and microbial spoilage. The incorporation of CQD led to clear improvements in functional performance, demonstrating how the inclusion of nanomaterials with specific properties can enhance film characteristics. This strategy may be extended to other materials—such as cellulose nanocrystals, starch NPs, or chitosan NPs—depending on functional needs and application contexts (D. Chen et al., 2025; Kashani Rahimi et al., 2017; Maghimaa et al., 2024). In addition to food applications, the biocompatibility, biodegradability, and photocatalytic functionality of these films suggest their potential utility in broader domains, such as biomedical packaging, environmental sensors, or agricultural coatings.

Gyumi Na: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jun-Won Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
